grounds.name: Improper purpose
grounds.plainName: The power was used for the wrong reason
grounds.test: A statutory power must be used for the purpose for which it was given; using it for another purpose may be unlawful — VERIFY the exact test + authorities against the corpus.

elements.name: Power used for a different purpose
elements.layPrompt: Does it seem the real reason for the decision was something other than what the power is for?

grounds.name: Procedural fairness (natural justice)
grounds.plainName: You weren't given a fair chance
grounds.test: A decision-maker must give a person a fair hearing and be free of bias — VERIFY the exact test + authorities against the corpus.

elements.name: Fair hearing
elements.layPrompt: Were you told what was being decided and given a real chance to respond before the decision was made?

elements.name: No bias
elements.layPrompt: Was there anything that suggests the decision-maker had already made up their mind, or had a personal interest?

grounds.name: Relevant and irrelevant considerations
grounds.plainName: They ignored something they had to weigh, or relied on something they shouldn't
grounds.test: A decision-maker must consider the matters the law requires, and must not rely on matters the law forbids — VERIFY the exact test + authorities against the corpus.

elements.name: Ignored a required consideration
elements.layPrompt: Was there something important the law required them to take into account that they seem to have ignored?

elements.name: Relied on an irrelevant consideration
elements.layPrompt: Did they rely on something that shouldn't have mattered to this decision?

grounds.name: Legal unreasonableness
grounds.plainName: The decision was one no reasonable decision-maker could make
grounds.test: A decision may be unlawful if it lacks an evident and intelligible justification, or is one no reasonable decision-maker could have reached — VERIFY the exact test + authorities against the corpus.

elements.name: No intelligible justification
elements.layPrompt: Does the outcome seem to make no sense on the facts the decision-maker had?

elements.name: Disproportionate or arbitrary
elements.layPrompt: Was the result far harsher than the situation called for, or seemingly arbitrary?

triage.mrVsJr: Merits review asks 'what is the correct or preferable decision?' — a tribunal stands in the decision-maker's shoes and can change the outcome. Judicial review asks 'was the decision lawful?' — a court checks the process and can set a decision aside, but cannot substitute its own. Choose by what the person wants: a different outcome points to merits review (if available); an unlawful process, or no merits-review right, points to judicial review. VERIFY the avenues for each decision against the data layer.

remedies.mr: affirm, vary, or set aside and substitute the decision (merits review)
remedies.jr: quash / set aside and send back to be remade; cannot substitute a new decision (judicial review)
